pattern: Splitting a sequence into two independent groups by index parity and sorting each group under its own rule is a bucket-then-recombine technique that shows up whenever a single pass rule secretly hides two unrelated sub-problems.
hints: The values that started at even positions and the values that started at odd positions never interact with each other, so pull them into two separate lists first.
One of those two lists wants ascending order and the other wants descending order; sort each list independently under its own rule.
Walk the output position by position, alternating which sorted list supplies the next value, so evens land back on even positions and odds land back on odd positions.

edge_inputs.description: single element, no odd index exists
already alternating in the target shape should stay stable
every value identical, sorting is a no-op either way
odd-length array leaves one extra even-index slot
maximum length with values at both bound extremes
descending input array to make sure ascending/descending rules aren't swapped
duplicate values spread across both parity groups

stress.description: random arrays across the full length and value range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 100) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def sortEvenOdd(self, nums):
        n = len(nums)
        evens = sorted(nums[0::2])
        odds = sorted(nums[1::2], reverse=True)
        result = [0] * n
        result[0::2] = evens
        result[1::2] = odds
        return result
